text: Here you can see the omentum inlay between the two edges of the sternum.
This is lying just on top of the pericardium.
First, we took measurements of the manubrial plates.
These were screwed in to stabilize each end of the bone using screws.
The bone allograft taken from our own bone bank here at Mayo Clinic.
We divided the bone graft with shears.
This was then inserted to bridge the defect.
Screws were then placed through the plate into the graft to keep them in place.
Here we're placing further bone grafts as we continue to work the face of the defect.
We then incorporated the use of an oscillating saw to help cut further pieces of bone.
Further plates were inserted all the way to the inferior portion of the wound.
This fixed the two edges of sternum together and the space in between was bridged with bone graft.
Here we are fixing the bone graft into place by adding further screws.
As you can see, the chest wall is quite stable now.
As we decided to add a few more plates, we fixed them first to the bone graft to make it easier for insertion.
Could allow easier insertion into tighter areas.
This is a test of the stability of the chest wall.
Pectoralis flaps were mobilized by plastic surgeons prior to the operation and they acted as overlay.
